title: It’s not our fault, says Labor
full_text:
Labor has been caught out attempting to minimise the market-breaking impact of its tax reforms by claiming low auction clearance rates are “predominantly” the fault of a 4.35 per cent interest rate, while changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax were merely a “small factor”.
However, from November 2023 to December 2024, when the cash rate was also at 4.35 per cent, the auction clearance rates in capital cities was 63.2 per cent, according to property analytics company Cotality.
By comparison, last weekend’s preliminary clearance rate was a six-year low of 49.2 per cent - a figure that is expected to drop further as the rates are finalised.
Opposition Leader Angus Taylor says Labor’s record spending is fuelling inflation and making life more expensive.
“They’re loading us up with tax and spending ... and that is hurting every one of us,” Mr Taylor said.
Government spending as a proportion of GDP continues to be at its highest levels outside of the pandemic, rising to 26.6 per cent of GDP in 2025-26.
But government MP Matt Thistlethwaite blamed the decline in auction clearances on the high cash rate, saying the tax changes were a “small factor”.
“We’ve had three increases in interest rates and there’s a bit of uncertainty about where the RBA will go next,” the Assistant Foreign Affairs Minister told Sky News.
“Obviously our tax changes ... are a small factor, but interest rates, I think it’s predominantly interest rates that are the reason.” Housing Minister Clare O’Neil has also previously said tax reforms were “not the main driver of what’s going on in the market”, taking responsibility instead for boosting the number of first homebuyers in the market.
On Sunday, Treasurer Jim Chalmers said there were a “whole range of factors which go into house prices and auction clearance rates”, including “interest rates, broader economic conditions, as well as tax settings”.
He also said conditions had softened prior to the federal budget on May 12. Ray White real estate chief economist Nerida Conisbee said confidence in the bottom end of the market had diminished, as evidenced by the clearance rates and the number of people at open home inspections halving.
“The budget happened in an already weakened market and it has accelerated the decline, and that’s really led to a high level of uncertainty, particularly because changing policies is impacting things,” she said.
“As a result, we’re seeing a lot of buyers sitting on the sidelines and just watching and waiting.” Long-term, she said “the pressure will shift onto rents”.
Cotality executive research director Tim Lawless said while it was difficult to tell how much the budget had contributed to the low clearance rates, it had “firmly” resulted in “some downside impact to housing conditions”.
“Spring will be a real test for the market, especially if we see another rate hike between now and then,” he said.
He added that more homes on the market, mixed with lowered buyer demand could reduce house prices further, leaving vendors in a “new normal” where they won’t get the “same sort of price as what they would have yesterday”.
Buyers would, however, be able to “negotiate harder” and “buy better and at a more affordable price point”.
Shadow treasurer Tim Wilson said “confidence has collapsed”, accusing Mr Chalmers and Prime Minister Anthony Albanese of “attacking the foundations of Australia’s economic prosperity and growth”.
During question time yesterday, Ms O’Neil denied first homebuyers who bought before the budget would be plunged into negative equity.
Asked by Liberal Dan Tehan whether Labor knew “how many first home buyers owe more money than their house is worth”, she said Treasury modelling indicated house prices would “continue to increase”, though the rate of growth would fall, adding: “We are talking about two percentage points different.” EDITORIAL PAGE 35
These ministers claim the current housing downturn is due to interest rate rises. History shows otherwise ■ Current cash rate 4.35% ■ Auction clearance rate 49.2% ■ Last time cash rate was 4.35% (Nov 2023 to Dec 2024) ■ Auction clearance rate 63.2%
